strengths: Novel claim-level memory schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges (contradict/supersede).
Session-batched writer + lexical gating keeps token cost 63 % below A-Mem while adding structure.
Explicit abstention policy: 80 % abstain rate on LongMemEval-S abstention set vs 0 % for Flat RAG.
Strict paired dominance on single-session-user slice (21/21 correct vs 19/21 Flat RAG).
Thorough ablation shows LLM linker is causal: removing it drops every targeted slice (ABS ‑22 pp, KU ‑9 pp).
Open release: full prompts, code, seeds, per-question outputs, and statistical scripts for reproducibility.
weaknesses: Overall accuracy statistically ties Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68.0 %, p=0.21) and is numerically lower.
Router compositions still underperform Flat RAG and sit 12–15 pp below oracle upper bound (82 %).
Intent classifier recall low on multi-session (42.5 %) and knowledge-update (39.1 %) slices, limiting router gain.
Regressions on SSA/SSP/MS slices traced to over-abstention from sparse assistant-side claim extraction.
Evaluation powered only for large deltas; key slices (ABS n=9, SSU n=21) lack conventional significance (p≥0.125).
Full 500-question LongMemEval-S result for TTMG deferred to camera-ready; current claim rests on 150-Q subset.
required_revisions: Run and report TTMG on the complete 500-question LongMemEval-S set with confidence intervals.
Provide power analysis or expand stratified subsets (especially ABS and SSU) to reach conventional significance.
Ablate the intent classifier itself: report router performance with a perfect-classifier oracle to quantify ceiling.
Detail failure-mode fixes for F1–F3 (e.g., denser assistant claim extraction or multi-claim reader composition).
Clarify statistical interpretation: discuss why overall parity is acceptable despite 6.7 pp absolute drop.
Release linker gate thresholds and subject–predicate overlap heuristics for full reproducibility.

strengths: Novel claim-level temporal memory schema with explicit contradict/supersede edges
Strict set-inclusion win on single-session-user slice (100 % vs 90.5 %) and large abstention behaviour gap (80 % explicit abstain vs 0 %)
Thorough ablation showing LLM linker is causal (-2.6 pp overall, -22 pp abstention)
Reproducible release: full prompts, seeds, per-question outputs, McNemar/Bootstrap scripts
Lower token cost than A-Mem (-63.5 %) while adding truth-maintenance semantics
weaknesses: Overall accuracy statistically ties Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68.0 %, p=0.21) and full abstention set also ties (76.7 % each)
Small-slice power: SSU n=21 (p=0.50), ABS n=9 (p=0.125 two-sided) underpowered for strong claims
Router classifiers have low recall on key slices (MS 42.5 %, KU 39.1 %) leaving 12-15 pp on table vs oracle
Writer under-extracts assistant-authored claims (7/7 SSA errors) and multi-claim aggregations (8/8 MS errors)
No cross-encoder reranker or ensemble ablation; retrieval budget fixed at k=8/3
required_revisions: Run powered comparison on full 500-question LongMemEval-S (already promised for camera-ready)
Add confidence-adjusted McNemar or aggregate slices to reach α=0.05 for SSU/ABS claims
Ablate retrieval k, reranker, and hybrid raw-turn budget to separate schema from retrieval effects
Report inter-annotator agreement or LLM linker accuracy to validate edge-label quality
Include statistical test for token-cost reduction (currently only point estimate)
Clarify whether DeepSeek-V3.2 reader is deterministic and if outputs were single-run

strengths: Claim-level schema with validity intervals and explicit contradict/supersede edges is a clean abstraction for truth maintenance
Session-batched writer + lexical gate keeps LLM linker off the hot path; 63 % token reduction vs A-Mem
Greedy max-consistent-subgraph filter is O(k²) and deterministic—cheap enough for real-time use
Router compositions recover Flat-RAG overall accuracy while preserving abstention behaviour on targeted slices
Full prompts, seeds, and per-question outputs released—reproducible by design
weaknesses: Overall accuracy statistically ties Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68.0 %, p=0.21); gains limited to two small slices
Intent classifier recall on MS/KU only 42 %/39 %—router leaves 12–15 pp on the table vs oracle
Writer skips assistant-authored facts (F1 mode) and multi-claim aggregates (F3), causing 18 paired regressions
Evaluation still on 150-question subset; authors admit power too low for McNemar significance on key slices
No latency breakdown for linker vs reader; end-to-end 85 s is workable but hardware not specified
required_revisions: Run the promised N=500 full-benchmark replication and report exact token & latency per component
Quantify linker gate hit-rate and average LLM calls per session; show this stays ≪ A-Mem per-turn cost
Release the subject–predicate lexical-check code and thresholds so gate speed can be reproduced
Add confidence-weighted abstention calibration curve—current 80 % abstain on _abs set looks hand-tuned
Discuss worst-case memory growth: number of claims, edges, and index size after months of real traffic

strengths: Conceptually clean framing of agent memory as a truth-maintenance problem rather than pure retrieval
Honest reporting: explicitly acknowledges regressions on SSA, SSP, MS slices and underpowered statistical tests
Ablation cleanly isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of gains on targeted slices
Efficiency gains over A-Mem are substantial (-63.5% tokens) and well-documented
Regression taxonomy (F1-F3) provides actionable future-work directions
Reproducibility commitment: promises code, seeds, per-question outputs, and frozen model identifiers
weaknesses: TTMG overall accuracy (61.3%) is numerically LOWER than Flat RAG (68.0%); claiming 'statistical parity' masks a negative-direction result
Primary wins are on severely underpowered slices: SSU n=21 (paired 2-0, p=0.50), ABS n=9 (paired 4-0, p=0.125)
Full abstention set (n=30) shows NO accuracy advantage for TTMG (both 76.7%); the 'behavioral difference' (explicit abstention) does not improve outcomes
Deployable routers (67.3%, 66.0%) still do not beat Flat RAG (68.0%) overall
Only 150 of 500 questions evaluated; full-scale TTMG run deferred to 'camera-ready future work'
A-Mem baseline is a reimplementation, not the original system; potential implementation quality gap
No comparison to any other structured/temporal memory system beyond A-Mem
Maximum-consistent-subgraph algorithm is greedy with only a weak 1/Δ approximation bound; no empirical comparison to exact solution
Intent classifier accuracy (64.7%) is low; oracle gap (82% vs 67.3%) shows current routing is far from optimal
required_revisions: Run and report TTMG on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S to confirm subset findings generalize
Report effect sizes (Cohen's d or similar) alongside p-values for underpowered slices, or explicitly state the study is underpowered and treat directional results as exploratory
Clarify that overall accuracy difference (61.3% vs 68.0%) trends against TTMG rather than framing as 'statistical parity' without acknowledging the direction
Compare A-Mem reimplementation against original A-Mem reported numbers to validate baseline fidelity
Add at least one additional structured memory baseline (e.g., MemoryBank, MemGPT) to contextualize TTMG's tradeoffs
Report per-question latency breakdown (writer vs linker vs reader) rather than only end-to-end, to verify the efficiency claim's source

strengths: Honest and transparent statistical reporting: McNemar p-values, bootstrap CIs, and explicit power analysis for underpowered slices
Well-structured failure-mode taxonomy (F1–F3) clearly explains regressions on SSA, SSP, MS slices
Single clean ablation (--contradict) isolates the LLM linker's causal role; every slice drops
Oracle upper-bound analysis (82.0%) quantifies complementarity gap between claim-level and raw-text retrieval
Efficiency gains over A-Mem are concrete and substantial (-63.5% tokens)
Reproducibility commitment: code, prompts, seeds, per-question outputs, frozen model identifiers
weaknesses: Headline wins are statistically underpowered: SSU paired 2-0 (p=0.50), ABS n=9 paired 4-0 (p=0.125); not conventionally significant
Abstention advantage disappears at full scale: n=30 ABS yields b=1,c=1 (p=1.00); TTMG and Flat are indistinguishable on outcome accuracy
Overall accuracy numerically lower (61.3% vs 68.0%) even if not statistically significant; TTMG regresses on 3 of 6 slices
A-Mem baseline is a reimplementation, not the original code—could be weaker than the published system
Only one ablation; missing ablations of claim schema components (validity intervals, polarity, volatility), hybrid reader, subject-predicate gate, max-consistent-subgraph vs simpler filter
Router compositions do not preserve abstention benefit: both routers' ABS accuracy equals Flat's 44.4%, not TTMG's 88.9%
Intent classifier accuracy only 64.7%; MS recall 42.5%—router is quite weak
TTMG not evaluated on full 500-question set; representativeness verified only for Flat RAG
required_revisions: Run TTMG on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S to verify subset representativeness for TTMG itself, not just Flat RAG
Add ablations for individual claim-schema components (validity intervals, polarity, volatility, confidence) and the hybrid reader fallback to demonstrate which design choices matter
Report effect sizes (e.g., Cohen's h or risk difference with CIs) alongside p-values for underpowered slices so readers can assess practical significance
Clarify why the routers fail to preserve abstention benefit and propose a concrete fix (e.g., abstention detector as a third routing signal) rather than leaving it as future work
Compare against the original A-Mem implementation rather than a reimplementation, or provide evidence that the reimplementation matches published numbers on A-Mem's own benchmarks

Address the n=30 abstention result explicitly in the abstract and introduction: the abstention accuracy advantage does not hold at full scale, only the behavioral difference (explicit abstention) does

strengths: Truth-maintenance framing for agent memory is conceptually valuable and under-studied
Clean ablation isolating linker's causal role; every slice drops when removed
Honest regression taxonomy (F1-F3) with structural root-cause analysis
Session-batched writer is practical; 63.5% token reduction vs A-Mem
Rigorous statistical reporting: McNemar tests, bootstrap CIs, power analysis acknowledged
Explicit abstention is a desirable safety property even without accuracy gain
weaknesses: TTMG overall accuracy (61.3%) is numerically worse than Flat RAG (68.0%); direction is negative
Claimed wins are on tiny underpowered slices: n=9 ABS (p=0.125), n=21 SSU (p=0.50)
Full n=30 abstention set shows zero accuracy difference (76.7% each); behavioral difference only
85s end-to-end latency per question is impractical for production deployment
Only 3 sessions ingested per question undermines long-horizon claim
N=500 evaluation missing for TTMG; only Flat RAG verified at scale
Regressions on SSA/SSP/MS are structural to claim-as-atom, not fixable without fallback
Router composition essentially defaults to Flat RAG for most questions, admitting TTMG alone insufficient
required_revisions: Run TTMG on full N=500 LongMemEval-S to verify subset representativeness claim
Report latency breakdown (writer/linker/parser/reader) and discuss production feasibility at 85s/question
Justify or relax the 3-session ingestion cap; evaluate with more sessions to test long-horizon scalability
Increase sample size on ABS and SSU slices to achieve statistical power (current n=9 and n=21 are underpowered)
Compare against original A-Mem implementation rather than reimplementation to ensure fair baseline
Report throughput (questions/hour) under concurrent load to validate systems claims
